reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' sections with concrete, distinct scenarios — not circular definitions. Both are peer H2s, which is structurally correct.

The handler code example correctly demonstrates the mandatory base64+gzip decode step, which is the single most important thing a first-time reader needs to know. Including it as working TypeScript prevents a guaranteed failure on first deploy.

The filter pattern table (literal string, exclusion, JSON equality, numeric comparison) is high-signal and saves the reader from immediately bouncing to AWS docs. Realistic patterns tied to concrete use cases.

The 'Throughput and delivery' section explicitly covers batching, the two-subscription-filter limit, retry/idempotency, and ordering — all grounded in the source interface and real AWS behavior. This is exactly the kind of production-relevant nuance a first-time user would miss.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (filter syntax, $ResourceParam), AWS-service questions (latency, subscription filter limit), and comparison questions (CloudWatch Logs vs alarms, trigger vs managed forwarding). Nine entries, no padding.

Code examples use proper <CodeBlock intellisense> format with focus markers, correct class names following the Integration pattern, and imports hidden from the rendered snippet. No forbidden YAML blocks, no fenced TypeScript config blocks.
problems: Missing <ApiReference definitionName="CloudwatchLogIntegrationProps" /> — the page rhythm explicitly requires exactly one ApiReference on every resource/trigger page. Its absence means the reader has no in-page way to discover all available properties, and it violates the completeness backbone. Completeness penalized -2.

FAQ answer for 'Can I use this trigger with batch jobs?' claims CloudwatchLogIntegration is available in the events array of batch jobs. The provided source file does not contain this claim — the CloudwatchLogIntegration interface JSDoc says 'Triggers a function when new log records appear in a CloudWatch log group', and nothing in events.d.ts ties it to batch jobs. This is an ungrounded Stacktape-specific claim.

The prose reference to 'log forwarding' (/observability/log-forwarding) in both the 'When NOT to use' section and the FAQ appears twice but the page route is not verified by the provided source files. If the route does not exist, these will be broken links — at minimum flag as a caveat during production review.
mandatoryFixes: Add exactly one <ApiReference definitionName="CloudwatchLogIntegrationProps" /> to the page. The conventional position is after the last configuration section (before or after the FAQ). Without it, the reader has no structured way to discover the complete property set, and the page violates the backbone rhythm required for all resource and trigger pages.

Remove or rephrase the batch job FAQ answer. The claim that CloudwatchLogIntegration is available in batch job events arrays is not grounded in the provided source (events.d.ts). Either drop the Q&A entirely, or replace it with a grounded question whose answer can be drawn from the source files. Do not assert Stacktape-specific behavior that the source does not support.
optionalImprovements: The prose hint about $ResourceParam() for referencing same-stack log groups ('use the $ResourceParam() directive to resolve the ARN at deploy time') is mentioned but never shown. A one-line inline snippet or a link to a concrete directive example would help first-time users who don't know how directives work.

The 'Throughput and delivery' section's subscription-filter limit paragraph recommends fanning out via SQS, SNS, or EventBridge but does not tell the reader which is usually the right choice. A one-sentence opinionated recommendation (e.g., 'SNS is the simplest fan-out if all consumers should receive every record; SQS is better if you need independent per-consumer retries') would complete the decision support.

Consider moving the Warning callout (base64+gzip is mandatory) directly into the 'Event payload' section's opening paragraph rather than after the code example. Readers who skim code first may miss it at the bottom — front-loading the gotcha reduces the most common failure mode.

The second <CodeBlock> (filtering logs example) duplicates the full LambdaFunction scaffolding from the first example. For a narrow-scope trigger page like this one, a shorter focused snippet showing only the integration object (with a prose note that it slots into the events array of a LambdaFunction) would reduce noise and make the filter property stand out more clearly.

optionalImprovements: The 'Retries' and 'Ordering' bullets in the Throughput and delivery section make behavioral claims (retries on error, per-invocation timestamp ordering) that go beyond what the source file states. reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

Revise the Throughput and delivery section to only state behavior grounded in the source, or explicitly narrow AWS-background claims. Remove or soften unsupported claims about seconds-level latency, subscription-filter limits, retries, duplicate delivery, and ordering if no source is added.
Remove or source the claim that CloudwatchLogIntegration is available on batch jobs. If the page keeps that FAQ answer, add source showing BatchJob events accept CloudwatchLogIntegration.

problems: The FAQ answer for 'Can I use this trigger with batch jobs?' states 'The CloudwatchLogIntegration is available in the events array of both Lambda functions and batch jobs.' The logGroupArn values in all three CodeBlock examples use the pattern arn:aws:logs:eu-west-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/lambda/my-app:* with a trailing :* wildcard. Standard CloudWatch log group ARNs do not end in :* — that suffix is IAM policy syntax. A developer copy-pasting this value into a real deployment will get an ARN validation error or an unintended subscription filter. The correct form is arn:aws:logs:eu-west-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/lambda/my-app without the :* suffix.

The 'Referencing log groups within a stack' section claims that 'Lambda functions, batch jobs, web services, worker services, and multi-container workloads all expose logGroupArn as a referenceable parameter.' reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
